title: Web Application Security
description: A compact starter course on high-interest security concepts that matter across PHP, Node, Python, Java, and cloud-hosted applications.
overview: Security is not a switch. It is a spectrum of trade-offs between convenience, performance, and risk reduction.
The goal of this pilot module is to prove the learning platform: readable lessons, short recall prompts, and spaced repetition that actually helps memory stick.
This first version is public and free, with browser-based progress storage so you can test the experience without accounts.

lessons.title: Input Validation
lessons.summary: Treat all incoming data as untrusted until it passes the right checks.
points: Validate on the server even if you also validate in the browser.
Check required fields for presence, length, type, and acceptable format.
Use allow-lists when you can. Reject values that do not match known-good patterns.

lessons.title: SQL Injection
lessons.summary: Unsafe string building can let attackers change the meaning of database queries.
points: Concatenating raw user input into SQL is dangerous.
Prepared statements separate data from query structure.
Validation and least-privilege database accounts reduce impact.

lessons.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
lessons.summary: Unescaped output can cause the browser to execute attacker-controlled script.
points: Escape output for the context where it will be rendered: HTML, attribute, URL, JavaScript, etc.
Stored XSS is especially dangerous because one bad payload can hit many users.
Content Security Policy helps, but it is not a substitute for proper output encoding.

lessons.title: Session Security
lessons.summary: Session identifiers must be protected like credentials.
points: Use secure cookies and HTTPS so session identifiers are not exposed in transit.
Regenerate session identifiers after login or privilege change.
Set sensible expiration rules and invalidate sessions on logout.

lessons.title: Password Hashing
lessons.summary: Passwords should be hashed with a slow, modern algorithm rather than encrypted or stored in plain text.
points: Use purpose-built password hashing such as bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2.
Password reset flows must be secure too, not just the login form.

cards.prompt: What is the golden rule for data received by a web application?
cards.answer: Treat all incoming data as untrusted or invalid until it passes the right validation checks.
cards.explanation: Client-side validation improves usability, but server-side validation is the real security boundary.

cards.prompt: Which is safer when validating expected values for a field like account role?
choices: Accept anything except obviously bad characters
Allow only known-good values such as admin, editor, user
Skip validation if JavaScript already checked it
cards.answer: Allow only known-good values such as admin, editor, user.
cards.explanation: Allow-lists are usually safer than trying to block every bad input pattern.

cards.prompt: What is the main defense against SQL injection in application code?
cards.answer: Use prepared statements or parameterized queries so user input is treated as data, not executable SQL.
cards.explanation: Escaping helps in some cases, but parameterized queries are the primary defense.

cards.prompt: Why is building SQL with raw string concatenation dangerous?
choices: It makes queries run slower on all databases
It can let attacker input alter the structure or meaning of the query
It prevents indexes from being used
cards.answer: It can let attacker input alter the structure or meaning of the query.
cards.explanation: The danger is not just bad data. The attacker may be able to change the logic of the query itself.

cards.answer: XSS happens when untrusted data is sent to a browser without proper output encoding, allowing script execution in the page context.
cards.explanation: Validation helps, but output encoding for the correct context is the key control.

cards.prompt: When should a session identifier usually be regenerated?
choices: Only when a password is changed
cards.explanation: This reduces the risk of session fixation and keeps privilege boundaries tighter.

cards.prompt: Why should passwords be hashed with a slow algorithm such as bcrypt or Argon2?
cards.answer: Slow password hashing makes large-scale brute-force and offline cracking attacks much more expensive.
cards.explanation: Password hashing is intentionally slower than general-purpose hashing because security matters more than raw speed here.

choices: Encrypted passwords are always better than hashed passwords
Plain text passwords are acceptable if the database server is private
Password reset workflows must be secured too, not just login
cards.answer: Password reset workflows must be secured too, not just login.
cards.explanation: Attackers often target the weaker recovery path instead of the stronger login path.
